title: Insoluble, but no precipitate? Dilute magnesium and hydroxide
scenario: Milk of magnesia is a suspension of solid **magnesium hydroxide**, Mg(OH)2 — the textbook “insoluble” base. So surely mixing a magnesium salt with a hydroxide makes the solid appear? Stir 50.0 mL of 0.00010 M Mg(NO3)2 (magnesium nitrate) into 50.0 mL of 0.00010 M NaOH — both perfectly clear. The dissolution equilibrium is $\mathrm{Mg(OH)_2(s)} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{Mg^{2+}(aq)} + 2\,\mathrm{OH^-(aq)}$, and whether a solid forms is decided by the **reaction quotient**: dilute each ion into the combined 100.0 mL, evaluate $Q = [\mathrm{Mg^{2+}}][\mathrm{OH^-}]^2$, and compare it to $K_{sp}$. If $Q < K_{sp}$ the solution is **unsaturated** and stays clear — however “insoluble” the compound is called.

assumptions.claim: Both source salts are soluble strong electrolytes that dissociate completely, and volumes are additive — so mixing 50.0 mL and 50.0 mL dilutes each ion into the combined 100.0 mL. The spectators $\mathrm{Na^+}$ and $\mathrm{NO_3^-}$ take no part, and water's own $\mathrm{OH^-}$ (about $10^{-7}$ M) is negligible beside the added hydroxide.
Activities are approximated by molar concentrations (the ideal-dilute-solution model), which is why $Q$ and $K_{sp}$ are written with concentrations.
The thermodynamic prediction is taken to hold: $Q < K_{sp}$ means no solid forms (the solution is unsaturated), and nothing kinetic changes that.

misconception.claim: Magnesium hydroxide is a classic insoluble compound, so mixing any magnesium salt with hydroxide must produce a precipitate.
